persons.arguments_against: Strongest case against: the biography is initials-only ('A. W.'), no birth year is printed, no honours are shared (the deterministic honour check FAILed), and three McLelan biographies exist — so a same-name relative (a son also 'A. W.') is the live hypothesis. It fails because the agreement is not on one office but on a whole career arc of specific, senior, named posts in the right order and at the right offsets: Minister of Marine and Fisheries in the 1883-1886 lists (bio: 'appointed minister of marine and fisheries' in 1881), Postmaster-General in the 1888 list (bio: 'postmaster-general 1887'), and 'Lieut. Governor of Nova Scotia' from the 1889 list (bio: 'lieutenant-governor, Nova Scotia, 1888') — each appearing one edition after the bio's appointment date, exactly the expected print lag. There is only ONE McLelan in the entire staff-list corpus, and Canada had one Postmaster-General and one Lieutenant-Governor of Nova Scotia at a time. The honour FAIL is vacuous: neither the bio nor any staff-list row prints an honour, so there was nothing to compare.
persons.would_overturn: Evidence of a second A. W. McLelan in Canadian federal politics in the 1880s holding any of these unique offices, or proof the Senate rows (1878-79) belong to a different McLelan — though the bio's 'President of the privy council of Canada until 1881' makes pre-1881 federal service expected.

notes: Single bio, single stint; the surname is unique in the staff-list corpus. Three distinct senior offices (Marine and Fisheries, Postmaster-General, Lieutenant-Governor of Nova Scotia) each match the biography with the normal one-year edition lag. The early Senator rows (1878-79) are consistent with the bio's pre-1881 privy-council career.
